generated_by: scheduled-task workday-t10-marketing-subject-lines — assistant fallback (marketing-skills bridge unavailable from daemon shell: no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, no plugin root on disk)

input.intro: Every active Day14 build has a public URL anyone can watch in real time — commit shas, screenshots, daily one-paragraph customer updates at day14.us/builds/{customer-slug}. The issue argues that radical transparency is the strongest marketing move available to a one-person build studio: prospects keep asking 'is this real,' a live commit log answers in one scroll, agencies can only show curated decks, and every commit doubles as content. The competitor-copy fear is overstated — judgment is the moat, not the code.
input.audience: Operators evaluating builders for their own business (skeptical, burned-before), plus indie-hacker readers interested in productized-services marketing tactics.
input.voice: Operator, anti-jargon, anti-agency. Confident without bravado. No exclamation points. No 'we'. Signed '— Jack / Day14'.

variants.subject: Every Day14 build has a public URL. Here's why.
variants.rationale: Leads with the unusual, concrete fact. The 'here's why' tail makes the body the payoff — strong open-rate hook.

variants.subject: Agencies show a deck. I show the commits.
variants.rationale: Compresses the issue's central contrast into seven words. Verb-first on both sides. The kind of line that quotes well in a retweet.

variants.subject: Is this real? A live build-log answers in one scroll.
variants.rationale: Quotes the question prospects actually ask and previews the punchline. Curiosity gap closed on click — good for engaged-list opens.

variants.subject: The strongest marketing move for a one-person shop
variants.rationale: Promises a tactic, names the reader (one-person shop) in the subject. Reads like an operator's note to a peer.

variants.subject: Why I publish every commit on every build
variants.rationale: First-person, plain, specific. The 'why' frame promises an argument rather than a brag.
